# Sudoku Flow — PWA Setup & Deployment Guide

This is your complete guide from "I have the source code" to
"it's live on the internet as a PWA".

---

## What you have

```
sudoku-flow/
├── public/
│   └── icons/           ← app icons go here (generated by scripts/generate-icons.js)
├── scripts/
│   └── generate-icons.js  ← generates all icon sizes from code
├── src/
│   ├── main.jsx           ← React entry point
│   └── SudokuApp.jsx      ← your entire app
├── index.html             ← the HTML shell
├── package.json           ← dependencies
├── vite.config.js         ← build config + PWA plugin
└── README.md              ← this file
```

---

## Step 1: Install Node.js

If you don't have it yet: https://nodejs.org — download the LTS version.

Verify it worked:
```bash
node --version   # should print v18 or higher
npm --version    # should print v9 or higher
```

---

## Step 2: Install project dependencies

Open a terminal, navigate to this folder, run:

```bash
cd sudoku-flow
npm install
```

This downloads React, Vite, and the PWA plugin into a `node_modules` folder.
That folder is large (~200MB) and never gets committed to git or deployed.

---

## Step 3: Generate icons

The app needs icons in 6 different sizes. A script generates them all:

```bash
npm install canvas    # one-time install of the icon drawing library
node scripts/generate-icons.js
```

You should see:
```
✓ Generated icon-16.png (16×16)
✓ Generated icon-32.png (32×32)
✓ Generated icon-180.png (180×180)
✓ Generated icon-192.png (192×192)
✓ Generated icon-512.png (512×512)
✓ Generated icon-512-maskable.png (512×512 maskable)
```

Open `public/icons/` in Finder/Explorer and check they look correct.

> **Tip:** If you want a custom icon design, replace these generated PNG files
> with your own. The sizes and filenames must match exactly.

---

## Step 4: Run locally

```bash
npm run dev
```

Open http://localhost:5173 in your browser.
The app should look and work exactly the same as it did in Claude.

> **Note:** The service worker does NOT run in dev mode — that's intentional.
> Service workers in dev cause confusing caching bugs. They only activate
> in the production build.

---

## Step 5: Build for production

```bash
npm run build
```

This creates a `dist/` folder. That folder is your entire app, compiled and
optimised. It contains:
- `index.html` — the shell
- `assets/` — your JS and CSS, fingerprinted for cache busting
- `sw.js` — the auto-generated service worker
- `manifest.webmanifest` — the PWA manifest
- `icons/` — all your app icons

Test the production build locally before deploying:
```bash
npm run preview
```
Open http://localhost:4173. This is exactly what users will see.

---

## Step 6: Deploy to the internet

### Option A — Netlify (recommended for beginners, free tier)

1. Create a free account at https://netlify.com
2. In your terminal:
   ```bash
   npm install -g netlify-cli
   netlify login
   netlify deploy --prod --dir=dist
   ```
3. Netlify gives you a URL like `https://sudoku-flow-abc123.netlify.app`

That's it. Your PWA is live.

### Option B — Vercel (also free, slightly faster CDN)

1. Create a free account at https://vercel.com
2. In your terminal:
   ```bash
   npm install -g vercel
   vercel login
   vercel --prod
   ```
   When it asks for the output directory, enter: `dist`

### Option C — GitHub Pages (free, but requires a GitHub account)

1. Push your code to a GitHub repo
2. Go to repo Settings → Pages → Source → GitHub Actions
3. Create `.github/workflows/deploy.yml`:
   ```yaml
   name: Deploy
   on:
     push:
       branches: [main]
   jobs:
     build-deploy:
       runs-on: ubuntu-latest
       steps:
         - uses: actions/checkout@v4
         - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
           with: { node-version: '20' }
         - run: npm install && npm run build
         - uses: peaceiris/actions-gh-pages@v3
           with:
             github_token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
             publish_dir: ./dist
   ```

---

## Step 7: Test your PWA

Once deployed, test these things:

### Test 1: Install prompt (Android Chrome)
1. Open your URL in Chrome on Android
2. After ~30 seconds of use, Chrome shows "Add to Home Screen" banner
3. Install it — it should open without browser chrome (no URL bar)

### Test 2: Install prompt (iOS Safari)
1. Open your URL in Safari on iPhone
2. Tap the Share button → "Add to Home Screen"
3. Opens as standalone app with your icon

### Test 3: Offline mode
1. Install the app on your phone
2. Turn on airplane mode
3. Open it — the app should still load and work fully
4. Play a puzzle — everything should function (it uses localStorage)

### Test 4: Lighthouse audit
1. Open your URL in Chrome on desktop
2. Open DevTools (F12) → Lighthouse tab
3. Run a PWA audit
4. You should score 90+ on PWA, Performance, and Accessibility

---

## Custom domain (optional but recommended)

A real domain ($10–15/year) makes the app look professional and is required
for the Google Play Store TWA submission later.

1. Buy a domain at Namecheap or Cloudflare Registrar
   - Good options: `sudokuflow.app`, `playsudukoflow.com`, `sudoku-flow.app`
2. In Netlify: Site Settings → Domain Management → Add custom domain
3. Follow Netlify's DNS instructions (takes ~10 minutes to propagate)

---

## What the PWA plugin does automatically

When you run `npm run build`, the `vite-plugin-pwa` plugin:

1. **Generates `sw.js`** — a service worker that:
   - Pre-caches all your JS, CSS, and HTML on first visit
   - Serves cached files instantly on repeat visits
   - Makes the app work with no internet connection
   - Updates silently in the background when you push new code

2. **Injects the manifest link** into `index.html` automatically

3. **Handles cache invalidation** — when you deploy a new version,
   the service worker detects the change and updates itself.
   Users get the new version on their next app open.

---

## Troubleshooting

**"npm install fails"**
Make sure you're inside the `sudoku-flow` folder when running the command.

**"The app looks wrong after building"**
Run `npm run preview` first to test locally before deploying.

**"Service worker not updating"**
Clear your browser's cache, or open DevTools → Application → Service Workers
→ click "Unregister", then refresh.

**"iOS doesn't show Add to Home Screen"**
You must be using Safari on iOS. Chrome on iOS cannot install PWAs (Apple
restriction). The `apple-mobile-web-app-capable` meta tag in index.html
handles this.

**"Lighthouse PWA score is low"**
The most common reasons:
- Not served over HTTPS (Netlify/Vercel do this automatically)
- Icons are missing or wrong size
- Manifest `start_url` doesn't match the deployed URL

---

## Next steps after this

Once the PWA is live and tested:

1. **Capacitor** — wrap it as a native iOS/Android app for the App Store
2. **AdMob** — add ads between puzzles
3. **RevenueCat** — paid theme unlocks
4. **Push notifications** — daily streak reminders

Each of those is a separate guide. Take them one at a time.
